Farn Polrrrcnl PRACTTcEs CounrssroN
.l?8 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramenro, CA gigl4-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
October 1,2009
Phaedra A Norton
City Attomey
The City of Turlock
156 S. Broadway, Suite 240
Turlock, CA 95380-5454
Re:
Your Advice Request
Our File No. A-09-209
Dear Ms. Norton:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding Mayor JohnLazar and his
obligations under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Politicaf Reform Act (the ,.Act',).1
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on
the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflicts of
interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. Furthermore, the Fair Political practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it issues advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, in rendering this advice, we rely solely on rhe
information you have provided to us.
QUESTION
Does the Mayor of Turlock have one or more conflicts of interest preventing him from
voting to approve or disapprove funding for the Turlock High Schoot pooiba[ Stadium?
CONCLUSIONS
The Mayor is presumed to have a conflict of interest based on his ownership of real
property within 500 feet of the stadium.
Furthermore, the Mayor may have another conflict of interest based on the renter of the
property because the renter is a source of income to the Mayor. Finally, the Mayor may have a
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
let'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the iair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Califrlrnia Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


Our File No. 4-09-209
Page2
conflict of interest in the potential effects of the decision on his personal finances. However, we
do not have sufficient facts to fully advise on these issues.
FACTS
The Turlock Redevelopment Agency is going to adopt its five year implementation plan.
As a part of the plan, the Turlock City Council identifies projects for ridevelopment. The
projects will receive funding through the Redevelopment Agency. One of the potential projects
that may receive such funding is the Turlock City High School Football Stadium (the,.siadium").
The stadium may be allocated up to $2 million in funding.
John Lazar, the Mayor of the city of Turlock, owns real property within 500 feet of the
stadium. The Mayor utilizes the property as a rental unit. The property is rented by an
individual. As you explained during our telephone conversation, the I\iayor's property is valued
at $2,000 or more, and the Mayor receives $500 or more in rental income ou"iu l2-month
period.
ANALYSIS
California Govemment Code Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or using his or her official position toinfluence a govemmental decision
in which they know or have reason to know that they have a financial interest. The Commission
uses an eight-step process for determining whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of
interest in a governmental decision. (Regulation 1g700(b)(l)-(g).)
Steps One & two: Is the Mayor a public oflicial who will make, participate in
making, or use his oflicial position to influence a goyernmental decisionf
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) The Act defines "public official" as "every member, oificer, employee or consultant of
a state or local government agency." (Section 820a8(a).) As city rnuyor, JoirnLazar is a public
official.
The Act provides that no public official shall "make, participate in making or in any way
use or attempt to use his/her official position to influence a governmental decision in whicl
he/she knows or has reason to know he/she has a disqualifying conflict of interest.,' (Section
87100; Regulation 18700.) These terns are clarified in regulations that have been adopted by
the Commission. (Regulations 1 8702- I 8 7 02.4.)
Making a Governmental Decision: A public official is "making a govemmental decision"
when, while acting within the authority of his or her office or position, he or she votes on a
matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to a course of action, or enters
into a contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation lB7O2.l.)


Our File No. 4-09-209
Page 3
ln your letter you asked the Commission to advise you as to whether the Mayor may vote
to approve or disapprove funding for the stadium redevelopment project. Such a uote qualifies
as "making a governmental decision."
Step Three: What are the Mayorrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The Act provides five categories of economic interest (Section 87103; Regulation
r8703.1-.s.)
"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,OOO) or more, or if the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, or employee of the business entity or holds
any position of management.
"(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest
worth two thousand dollars ($2,OOO; or more.
"(c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public
without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred dollars ($5oo; oi-o."
in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
"(d) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts
aggregating four hundred twenty dollars ($4ZOy or more in value provided to,
received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.
"(e) A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family
(spouse or dependent child). This is commonly referred to as the ..personal
financial effects" ruIe."
Based on the facts provided in your advice request, it seems likely that the Mayor has
three economic interests regarding the stadium redevelopment plan. The first economic interest
is the Mayor's real property that is within 500 feet of the stadium. The second economic interest
is the Mayor's source of income economic interest. The third economic interest is the Mayor's
interest in his personal finances.
Real Property Interest: According to the facts, the Mayor owns a piece of real property
valued at $2,000 or more. Thus, the Mayor has an economic interest in this property. (Section
87r03(b).)


Our File No. ,\-09-209
Page 4
Sourc,e of Income Interest: A public official has an economic interest in a source of
income that aggregates $500 or more in value, either promised or received, by the official within
a 12 month period prior to the governmental decision. The facts provided to the Commission
indicate that the real property owned by the Mayor is utilized as a rental unit, and that the Mayor
receives $500 or more in rental income per 12 months. Under the Act, "income" includes reni
payments received by the public official. (Section 82030.) Thus, the Mayor has an economic
interest in the individual who renrs his real property. (Section 87103(c).)
Personal Financial Effect: A public official will always have an economic interest in his
or her personal finances and those of his or her immediate family. A governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) The Mayor has an economic interest in his personal
finances.
Step Four: Are the Mayor's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
"fn order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision. If a
public official's economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is
indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)
Real Property Interest: [n order to determine whether the Mayor's real property is directly
or indirectly involved in the governmental decision, we must consult Regulation L8704.2.
(Regul ation 187 }a@)Q).)
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a govemmental decision if any of the following apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision. For purposes of subdivision (aX5), real property
is located "within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of
the real property which is the subject of the govemmental decision" if any
part of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.
{<**
"(5) The govemmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the


Our File No. 4-09-209
Page 5
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area."
The real property owned by the Mayor is located within 500 feet of the proposed
redevelopment of the stadium. Thus, the Mayor's real property is directly invoived in the
decision.
Source of Income: In order to determine whether the Mayor's source of income is
directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision, we must consult Regulation
18704.1. (Regulation 1 870a(a)( 1).)
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of
gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that
person, either directly or by an agent:"
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;"
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Based on the information provided to the Commission, it does not appear that the renter
qualifies as "directly involved" in the governmental decision. Therefore, the renter is "indirectly
involved" in the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(a).)
Personal Finances: A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be
directly involved in a governmental decision that has any financial effect on his or her personal
finances or those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 19704.5.)
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Step Five requires a determination of the applicable materiality standard. (Regulation
18700(bX5).) The applicable standard is determined by the type of conflict of interest.
(Regulation 18705.)
Real Property Interest: The materiality standard for economic interests in directly
involved real property is found in Regulation lB705.Z(a)(l):
"The financial effect of a governmental decision on the real property is
presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that


Our File No. 4-09-209
Page 6
it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have
any financial effect on the real property."
On this basis, the decision on whether to fund the stadium is presumed to have a material
effect on the Mayor's real property. The standard set by Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) is a strict one;
"any financial effect" includes a change in value of the real property as little as one cent. This is
known as the "one penny ruIe." Therefore, the financial effect of the stadium funding decision
on the Mayor's real property is presumed, but may be rebutted if it can be shown tharthe
decision will not affect the value of the real property by even one penny. Because you have not
provided us with any information that would support such a rebuttal, we will assume the
materiality standard has been met.
The following is the materiality standard for indirectly involved leaseholds in property.
(Regulation 18705.3(b)(3XB); Regutation 1 8705.2(b).)
"(2) Real property, leaseholds. The financial effect of a governmental decision
on real property in which a public official has a leasehold interest and which is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the
real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will:',
"(A) Change the legally allowable use of the leased real property, and the lessee
has a right to sublease the real property;
"(B) Change the lessee's actual use of the real property;
"(C) Substantially enhance or significantly decrease the lessee's use or
enjoyment of the leased real property;
"(D) lncrease or decrease the amount of rent for the leased real property by
5+percent during any l2-month period following the decision; or
"(E) Result in a change in the termination date of the lease.',
Thus, the materiality standard used for the Mayor's interest in the lease is based on the
possible effects of the stadium funding decision on the on the terms of the lease. Because you
have not provided us with the facts needed to make this determination, we cannot determine at
this time whether the stadium funding decision will meet the materiality standard for the Mayor's
interest in the lease. Therefore, absent more facts, we conclud" ou, anulysis here on whether the
stadium funding decision will have a material financial effect on the terms of the lease.
Source of Income Interest: As was discussed in our September 3'd telephone
conversation, we will assume that the renter of the Mayor's property is an individual for the


Our File No. 4-09-209
PageT
purposes of this letter. Please note that the applicable materiality standard is different for other
types of sources of income, such as business or non-profit entitiis. (Regulation 1g705.3.) The
materiality standards for indirectly involved sources of income are found in Regulation
18705.3(b):
"(3) sources of income who are individuals. The effect of a decision is
material as to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any
of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, sections I 8705.2(b)."
Thus, the materiality standard used for the Mayor's source of income from an individual
would examine the effects of the stadium funding decision on the income, personal finances, or
real property interest of that individual. Because you have not provided us with the facts needed
to make this determination, we cannot determine at this time whether the effects of the stadium
funding decision will meet this materiality standard. Therefore, absent more facts, we conclude
our analysis here on whether the stadium funding decision will have a material financial effect
on the source of income to the Mayor.
Personal Finances: Generally, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
personal finances, or those of his or her immediate family, of at Ieast $250 in any l1month
period is deemed material. However, this standard does not apply where, as pertinent here, the
financial effect is on the value of the official's real property. iherefore, tne $ZSO materiality
standard for the Mayor's personal finances will only apply to effects the stadium funding
decision may have on increases or decreases in personii expenses, income, assets, or liabilities
incurred in relation to matters other than increases or decreases to the Mayor,s real property
value.
Step Six: Is the effect upon the economic interest reasonably foreseeable?
"A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that economic
interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision." lRegulation 1g706(a).) A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foriseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.; Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the decision is
made depends on the facts surrounding the decisi on. (Gogna Advice Letter, No. A-09-l7l)


Our File No. 4-09-209
Page 8
Under the facts that you have provided, it seems likely that there will be a material
financial effect on the Mayor's economic interests as a result of the stadium funding decision due
to his ownership of real property within 500 feet of the stadium. Note that any financial effect
on the Mayor's property will be disqualifying. However, because there are iniufficient facts
concerning the effects of the decision on the Mayor's other economic interests, and the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice, we are unable to provide a
definitive answer to the foreseeability issue as it related to the Mayor's source of income and
personal finances economic interests.
Steps Seven & Eight: The "public generally" & "legally required participation,
exceptions.
The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision despite a
conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's intereit is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation lg7o7.)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances a public official may be called upon to take
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception appl[s only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyLid from acting. (Section
87101 ; Regulation I 8708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the ,.legally
required participation" exceptions are applicable to the Mayor, so we will not address them
further in this letter.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By:
RCjgl
Legal Division

purposes of this letter. Please note that the applicable materiality standard is different for other
types of sources of income, such as business or non-profit entitiis. (Regulation 1g705.3.) The
materiality standards for indirectly involved sources of income are found in Regulation
18705.3(b):
"(3) sources of income who are individuals. The effect of a decision is
material as to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any
of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, sections I 8705.2(b)."
Thus, the materiality standard used for the Mayor's source of income from an individual
would examine the effects of the stadium funding decision on the income, personal finances, or
real property interest of that individual. Because you have not provided us with the facts needed
to make this determination, we cannot determine at this time whether the effects of the stadium
funding decision will meet this materiality standard. Therefore, absent more facts, we conclude
our analysis here on whether the stadium funding decision will have a material financial effect
on the source of income to the Mayor.
Personal Finances: Generally, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
personal finances, or those of his or her immediate family, of at Ieast $250 in any l1month
period is deemed material. However, this standard does not apply where, as pertinent here, the
financial effect is on the value of the official's real property. iherefore, tne $ZSO materiality
standard for the Mayor's personal finances will only apply to effects the stadium funding
decision may have on increases or decreases in personii expenses, income, assets, or liabilities
incurred in relation to matters other than increases or decreases to the Mayor,s real property
value.
Step Six: Is the effect upon the economic interest reasonably foreseeable?
"A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that economic
interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision." lRegulation 1g706(a).) A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foriseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.; Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the decision is
made depends on the facts surrounding the decisi on. (Gogna Advice Letter, No. A-09-l7l)


Our Page 8
Under the facts that you have provided, it seems likely that there will be a material
financial effect on the Mayor's economic interests as a result of the stadium funding decision due
to his ownership of real property within 500 feet of the stadium. Note that any financial effect
on the Mayor's property will be disqualifying. However, because there are iniufficient facts
concerning the effects of the decision on the Mayor's other economic interests, and the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice, we are unable to provide a
definitive answer to the foreseeability issue as it related to the Mayor's source of income and
personal finances economic interests.
Steps Seven & Eight: The "public generally" & "legally required participation,
exceptions.
The "public generally" exception allows an official to participate in a decision despite a
conflict of interest if the effect of the decision on the official's intereit is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation lg7o7.)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances a public official may be called upon to take
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception appl[s only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyLid from acting. (Section
87101 ; Regulation I 8708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the ,.legally
required participation" exceptions are applicable to the Mayor, so we will not address them
further in this letter.

Broadway, Suite 240 Turlock, CA 95380-5454 Re: Your Advice Request Our File No. A-09-209 Dear Ms. Norton: This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding Mayor JohnLazar and his obligations under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Politicaf Reform Act (the ,.Act',).1 Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflicts of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. Furthermore, the Fair Political practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it issues advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, in rendering this advice, we rely solely on rhe information you have provided to us. QUESTION Does the Mayor of Turlock have one or more conflicts of interest preventing him from voting to approve or disapprove funding for the Turlock High Schoot pooiba[ Stadium? CONCLUSIONS The Mayor is presumed to have a conflict of interest based on his ownership of real property within 500 feet of the stadium. Furthermore, the Mayor may have another conflict of interest based on the renter of the property because the renter is a source of income to the Mayor. As a part of the plan, the Turlock City Council identifies projects for ridevelopment. The projects will receive funding through the Redevelopment Agency. One of the potential projects that may receive such funding is the Turlock City High School Football Stadium (the,.siadium"). The stadium may be allocated up to $2 million in funding. John Lazar, the Mayor of the city of Turlock, owns real property within 500 feet of the stadium. The Mayor utilizes the property as a rental unit. The property is rented by an individual.
